content:
# Luke Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 101-102 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_011_page_001.jpg, LUK_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** One day Jesus was praying in a certain place. When he finished, one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, just as John taught his disciples."

**2** He said to them, "When you pray, say: 'Father, may your name be honored as holy. May your kingdom come. **3** Give us each day the bread we need for that day. **4** And forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who wrongs us. And do not bring us into a time of testing.'"

**5** Then he said to them, "Imagine one of you has a friend, and you go to him at midnight and say, 'Friend, lend me three loaves of bread, **6** because a friend of mine has arrived after a journey and I have nothing to offer him.' **7** And suppose the one inside answers, 'Stop bothering me. The door is already locked, and my children and I are in bed. I cannot get up and give you anything.' **8** I tell you, even if he will not get up and give him anything because of their friendship, yet because of the man's bold persistence he will get up and give him as much as he needs.

**9** "So I tell you: Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will be opened for you. **10** For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened.

**11** "What father among you, if your son asks for a fish, would give him a snake instead? **12** Or if he asks for an egg, would hand him a scorpion? **13** If you, then, with all your flaws, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!"

**14** One day Jesus was driving out a demon that had made a man unable to speak. When the demon came out, the man who had been mute began to talk, and the crowds were amazed. **15** But some of them said, "He drives out demons by Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons." **16** Others, trying to test him, demanded a sign from heaven.

**17** But Jesus, knowing their thoughts, said to them, "Every kingdom torn apart by civil war is headed for destruction, and a house divided against itself collapses. **18** So if Satan is fighting against himself, how can his kingdom survive? I ask because you claim I drive out demons by Beelzebul. **19** And if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your own followers drive them out? They will be your judges on this point. **20** But if it is by the finger of God that I drive out demons, then the kingdom of God has arrived right here among you.

**21** "When a strong, fully armed man guards his own estate, his possessions are safe. **22** But when someone even stronger attacks and overpowers him, the stronger one strips away all the weapons the man trusted in and distributes his plunder.

**23** "Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.

**24** "When an unclean spirit leaves a person, it roams through waterless wastelands searching for a place to rest. Finding none, it says, 'I will go back to the house I left.' **25** When it arrives, it finds the house swept clean and put in order. **26** Then it goes and brings along seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they all move in and settle there. And that person ends up worse off than before."

**27** As he was saying these things, a woman in the crowd raised her voice and called out to him, "Blessed is the mother who gave you birth and nursed you!"

**28** But he replied, "Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and put it into practice."

**29** As the crowds were increasing, Jesus said, "This generation is an evil generation. It demands a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah. **30** For just as Jonah became a sign to the people of Nineveh, so also the Son of Man will be a sign to this generation. **31** The queen of the South will rise at the judgment alongside the people of this generation and condemn them, because she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon — and look, something greater than Solomon is here. **32** The people of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment alongside this generation and condemn it, because they turned their lives around when Jonah preached to them — and look, something greater than Jonah is here.

**33** "No one lights a lamp and then hides it or puts it under a basket. Instead, they place it on a stand so that those who come in can see the light. **34** Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eye is healthy, your whole body is full of light. But when your eye is diseased, your body is full of darkness. **35** Be careful, then, that the light inside you has not become darkness. **36** So if your whole body is full of light, with no part of it in darkness, it will be completely radiant — as when a lamp shines its light on you."

**37** When he had finished speaking, a Pharisee invited Jesus to eat with him. So he went in and reclined at the table. **38** The Pharisee was surprised to notice that Jesus did not first perform the ritual washing before the meal.

**39** Then the Lord said to him, "You Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and the dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness. **40** How foolish you are! Did not the one who made the outside also make the inside? **41** Instead, give what is inside to the poor, and then everything will be clean for you.

**42** "But how terrible it will be for you Pharisees! You give God a tenth of your mint, your rue, and every garden herb, but you neglect justice and the love of God. You should have practiced the latter without leaving the former undone.

**43** "How terrible it will be for you Pharisees! You love the most important seats in the synagogues and the respectful greetings you receive in the marketplaces.

**44** "How terrible it will be for you! You are like unmarked graves that people walk over without ever knowing it."

**45** One of the legal experts spoke up: "Teacher, when you say these things, you insult us too."

**46** Jesus replied, "How terrible it will be for you legal experts as well! You load people down with burdens they can hardly carry, yet you yourselves will not lift a single finger to help them.

**47** "How terrible it will be for you! You build elaborate tombs for the prophets, yet it was your ancestors who killed them. **48** So you are witnesses who approve of what your ancestors did: they killed the prophets, and you build their tombs. **49** This is why the Wisdom of God said, 'I will send them prophets and apostles; some of them they will kill and some they will persecute.' **50** As a result, this generation will be held accountable for the blood of all the prophets shed since the creation of the world — **51** from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes, I tell you, this generation will be held accountable for all of it.

**52** "How terrible it will be for you legal experts! You have taken away the key to knowledge. You did not go in yourselves, and those who were trying to enter, you blocked."

**53** When Jesus left that place, the scribes and Pharisees began to oppose him fiercely and to bombard him with questions on every subject, **54** plotting to trap him in something he might say.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **11:2 — "Father, may your name be honored as holy"**: The Sinaiticus text of the Lord's Prayer in Luke is notably shorter than the Matthean version (Matthew 6:9-13). It lacks "who is in heaven," "your will be done on earth as it is in heaven," and "but deliver us from evil." This is not a deficiency — Luke preserves an earlier, more compressed form. The address ΠΑΤΕΡ (Pater, "Father") without qualification reflects the startling intimacy that Jesus brought to prayer. In Aramaic, this would be Abba — an address so familiar that many rabbis considered it inappropriate for addressing God. Jesus makes it the first word of prayer.

- **11:2 — "May your kingdom come"**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΛΘΕΤΩ Η ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΣΟΥ. Some Western manuscripts substitute "May your Holy Spirit come upon us and purify us" — an intriguing variant that was not adopted by the broader manuscript tradition. The petition for the kingdom is not a request for escape from the world but a longing for God's reign to fully manifest on earth. It is both future hope and present invitation.

- **11:3 — "the bread we need for that day"**: The Greek ΕΠΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ (epiousios) is one of the most debated words in the New Testament because it appears virtually nowhere else in Greek literature. It may mean "daily," "for the coming day," "necessary for existence," or even "supernatural." The ambiguity may be intentional: the prayer asks for both physical sustenance and spiritual nourishment — whatever is needed to sustain life today, trusting God again tomorrow.

- **11:4 — "do not bring us into a time of testing"**: The Greek ΠΕΙΡΑΣΜΟΝ (peirasmon) can mean temptation, trial, or testing. The petition does not imply God tempts anyone (James 1:13) but acknowledges human fragility: we ask God not to lead us into situations where our faith would be overwhelmed. It is a prayer of honest self-awareness about our own weakness.

- **11:5-8 — The friend at midnight**: This parable is built on the cultural dynamics of Middle Eastern hospitality. When a guest arrives, the host is honor-bound to provide food — failure is a disgrace to the entire village, not just the individual. The sleeping neighbor knows this. The word ΑΝΑΙΔΕΙΑΝ (anaideian, translated "bold persistence") literally means "shamelessness" or "lack of shame." The man keeps knocking because he is unashamed to press his need. Jesus' point is not that God is a reluctant giver who must be pestered, but rather the reverse: if even a grumpy neighbor will eventually give in, how much more eagerly will God respond to those who ask boldly.

- **11:13 — "the Holy Spirit"**: This is a distinctly Lukan reading. Matthew's parallel (7:11) says "good things." Luke's substitution of "the Holy Spirit" makes explicit what is implicit: the ultimate good gift God gives is not any material blessing but his own presence. This verse stands as one of Luke's clearest statements about the availability of the Spirit to all who ask — not reserved for prophets and kings, but offered to anyone who prays.

- **11:15 — "Beelzebul"**: The name ΒΕΕΛΖΕΒΟΥΛ is a deliberate distortion of Baal-Zebub ("Lord of the Flies"), the god of Ekron (2 Kings 1:2), reshaped into "Baal-Zebul" meaning "Lord of the Dwelling/Dung." It had become a popular Jewish title for Satan or the chief of demons. The accusation was the most dangerous charge Jesus' opponents could level: not that his power was fake, but that it came from the wrong source.

- **11:17 — "a house divided against itself collapses"**: Jesus' logic is devastating in its simplicity. If Satan is empowering Jesus to destroy Satan's own agents, then Satan is waging war on himself — an absurd strategy. The argument exposes the accusation as not merely wrong but incoherent. A kingdom at war with itself cannot stand; everyone knows this. The accusers have not thought through their own claim.

- **11:20 — "by the finger of God"**: The phrase ΔΑΚΤΥΛΩ ΘΕΟΥ (daktulo theou, "finger of God") is a direct echo of Exodus 8:19, where Pharaoh's magicians, unable to replicate the plague of gnats, concede "this is the finger of God." Matthew's parallel reads "Spirit of God." Luke's choice of "finger" connects Jesus' exorcisms to the Exodus liberation — God is doing again what he did in Egypt, breaking the power of oppression. The kingdom is not a distant promise; it has arrived (ΕΦΘΑΣΕΝ, ephthasen — past tense, already here).

- **11:21-22 — The strong man parable**: The "strong man" is Satan, who has been guarding his domain — enslaved humanity — with confidence. The "someone stronger" is Jesus, who overpowers him, strips his armor, and redistributes his captives as freed people. This is not a negotiation or a close contest. It is an invasion by a superior power. The exorcisms are evidence that the strong man's house is being plundered right now.

- **11:24-26 — The return of the unclean spirit**: The parable warns that spiritual emptiness is dangerous. Deliverance that is not followed by filling — being swept clean but remaining vacant — creates vulnerability to worse invasion. The number seven represents completeness of evil. Jesus is not teaching that exorcism is futile but that liberation must be accompanied by new allegiance. A house emptied of evil must be filled with good.

- **11:27-28 — "Blessed is the mother who gave you birth"**: The woman's exclamation reflects a common Near Eastern blessing — praising a man by honoring his mother. Jesus does not reject the sentiment but redirects it: biological connection to him matters less than spiritual obedience. The truly blessed are not those with proximity to Jesus by blood but those who hear God's word and live by it. This theme pervades Luke (cf. 8:19-21).

- **11:29 — "the sign of Jonah"**: Unlike Matthew, who explicitly connects the sign to Jonah's three days in the fish (Matthew 12:40), Luke's Sinaiticus text leaves the comparison open. The sign is Jonah himself — as a preacher whose very person was a sign of God's message to Nineveh, so the Son of Man will be to this generation. The sign is not a spectacular miracle but the messenger himself, standing before them, demanding a response.

- **11:31 — "The queen of the South"**: The Queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1-10) traveled an enormous distance to hear Solomon's wisdom. The Ninevites repented at the preaching of a reluctant prophet. Both pagan figures responded to far less divine revelation than what this generation is receiving in Jesus. The argument is one of ascending shame: if they responded, what excuse does Israel have?

- **11:33 — "No one lights a lamp and then hides it"**: The lamp sayings connect the Jonah discourse to the Pharisee confrontation that follows. God's revelation is not hidden — it is placed on a stand for all to see. The problem is not the lamp but the eye. If your perception is warped, even abundant light cannot help you.

- **11:34 — "When your eye is healthy"**: The Greek ΑΠΛΟΥΣ (haplous) literally means "single" or "simple" — an undivided eye that sees clearly and generously. The opposite, ΠΟΝΗΡΟΣ (poneros, "evil" or "diseased"), suggests a stingy, envious, or distorted way of seeing. In Jewish idiom, a "good eye" meant generosity and an "evil eye" meant stinginess. Jesus is diagnosing the Pharisees' problem: their way of seeing reality is fundamentally distorted, which makes their whole existence dark even while they believe themselves enlightened.

- **11:38 — "did not first perform the ritual washing"**: The washing was not about hygiene but about ceremonial purity — a tradition of the elders, not a biblical commandment. Pharisees washed hands (and sometimes arms to the elbow) before meals as a fence around the Torah's purity laws. Jesus' deliberate omission was not carelessness; it was a provocation that set up the teaching that follows.

- **11:39 — "the outside of the cup and the dish"**: The metaphor strikes at the heart of Pharisaic religion: meticulous external compliance masking internal corruption. ΑΡΠΑΓΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΟΝΗΡΙΑΣ (harpages kai ponerias) — "greed and wickedness" — are not minor flaws. Jesus accuses these religious leaders of being predatory on the inside while polished on the outside. The cup looks clean to dinner guests, but God sees what fills it.

- **11:41 — "give what is inside to the poor"**: The Greek ΤΑ ΕΝΟΝΤΑ ΔΟΤΕ ΕΛΕΗΜΟΣΥΝΗΝ is difficult. It may mean "give the contents [of the cup] as charity" or "give from the heart." Either way, the remedy for inner greed is outward generosity. Almsgiving does not earn purity, but it demonstrates the transformation of the heart. True cleanness flows from the inside out.

- **11:42 — "mint, rue, and every garden herb"**: These were small household plants. Tithing them demonstrated extraordinary scrupulousness — the Pharisees were so precise that they counted leaves from their window-box herbs. Jesus does not condemn the practice itself ("without leaving the former undone") but exposes the grotesque imbalance: microscopic attention to herb-tithing alongside wholesale neglect of justice and love.

- **11:44 — "unmarked graves"**: Contact with a corpse or grave made a person ritually unclean for seven days (Numbers 19:16). Graves were typically whitewashed to make them visible so people could avoid contamination. An unmarked grave was a hidden source of defilement — people became unclean without knowing it. Jesus' accusation is blistering: the Pharisees, who consider themselves guardians of purity, are actually invisible sources of contamination to everyone they encounter.

- **11:46 — "burdens they can hardly carry"**: The Greek ΦΟΡΤΙΑ ΔΥΣΒΑΣΤΑΚΤΑ (phortia dysbastakta, "loads difficult to bear") refers to the accumulated weight of legal rulings that the experts piled onto ordinary people. The experts themselves found loopholes and exemptions but offered no help to those struggling under the load. Jesus indicts not the law itself but the hypocrisy of those who administer it without compassion.

- **11:47-48 — "You build elaborate tombs for the prophets"**: The irony is razor-sharp. The lawyers honor dead prophets with beautiful monuments while their living religious tradition perpetuates the same hostility that killed those prophets. Building a tomb implies distance from the crime — "we would never have done what our ancestors did" — but Jesus collapses that distance. By rejecting the current word of God, they prove themselves heirs of the same spirit that murdered the messengers.

- **11:49 — "the Wisdom of God said"**: This phrase introduces a quotation not found in any known Old Testament text. It may refer to a lost Jewish writing, or Jesus may be speaking as the personified Wisdom of God (cf. Proverbs 8, Sirach 24). In Matthew's parallel (23:34), Jesus says "I send you" directly. Luke's attribution to "the Wisdom of God" adds a cosmic dimension: this is not just Jesus' personal decision but the eternal plan of divine Wisdom unfolding through history.

- **11:51 — "from Abel to Zechariah"**: Abel was the first murder victim in Scripture (Genesis 4). Zechariah son of Jehoiada was killed "in the court of the house of the LORD" (2 Chronicles 24:20-22) — and 2 Chronicles is the last book in the Hebrew Bible's canonical order. So "from Abel to Zechariah" means from the first to the last martyrdom in the entire Hebrew Scriptures. This generation stands at the culmination of a long history of rejecting God's messengers, and the accumulated guilt has reached its crisis point.

- **11:52 — "the key to knowledge"**: The ΚΛΕΙΔΑ ΤΗΣ ΓΝΩΣΕΩΣ (kleida tes gnoseos) is the ability to unlock the meaning of Scripture and lead people to God. The legal experts were supposed to be doorkeepers who opened the way to understanding. Instead, they became bouncers who blocked the entrance — neither entering themselves nor allowing others through. This is the final and perhaps most damning of the woes: they have turned their sacred vocation into an instrument of exclusion.

- **11:53-54 — "oppose him fiercely"**: The Greek ΔΕΙΝΩΣ ΕΝΕΧΕΙΝ (deinos enechein) conveys intense, hostile pressure. The scribes and Pharisees begin ΑΠΟΣΤΟΜΑΤΙΖΕΙΝ (apostomatizein) — "to question from the mouth," meaning to provoke him into spontaneous statements they could use against him. The dinner party has become an interrogation. The religious establishment has moved from surprise to offense to active conspiracy. The conflict inaugurated in this chapter will not de-escalate — it will drive the narrative toward Jerusalem and the cross.
